sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A chemical kinetics lab measures the rate constant for a first-order reaction at several temperatures. The reaction has k = 0.020 s−1 at 290 K, 0.060 s−1 at 310 K, and 0.160 s−1 at 330 K. Students construct an Arrhenius plot and conclude that the rate constant increases with temperature because more molecules have enough energy to overcome the activation barrier.
questions.stem: How does the rate constant change as temperature increases?

explanations.D: Correct. The graph shows k rising from 0.020 s−1 at 290 K to 0.160 s−1 at 330 K. This is the expected Arrhenius temperature dependence.
explanations.A: The plotted values increase, not decrease.
explanations.B: The values change substantially with temperature.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Temperature dependence of the first-order rate constant.

questions.stem: What is the half-life at 310 K for this first-order reaction?

explanations.A: Correct. For a first-order reaction, t1/2 = 0.693/k. At k = 0.060 s−1, t1/2 ≈ 0.693/0.060 ≈ 11.6 s.
explanations.B: 0.060 s is the rate constant value, not the half-life.
explanations.C: 58 s is too long for this k and would correspond to a much smaller rate constant.
explanations.D: 1.4 s uses an incorrect inverse relationship.

questions.stem: Why does increasing temperature increase k?

choices.C: A larger fraction of molecules exceeds the activation energy
choices.A: The activation energy becomes exactly zero

explanations.C: Correct. Higher temperature shifts the molecular energy distribution so more collisions have enough energy to react. Activation energy need not disappear.
explanations.A: Activation energy does not become zero just because temperature rises.
explanations.B: Reaction order is determined by mechanism and rate law, not automatically by temperature.
explanations.D: Products are not described as catalysts in the passage.

questions.stem: For a first-order reaction, what are the units of k?

explanations.B: Correct. First-order rate laws have rate = k[A]. Since rate has units M/s and [A] has units M, k has units s−1.
explanations.A: M−1s−1 is typical for a second-order rate constant.
explanations.C: M/s is a rate unit, not k for first order.
explanations.D: s is the inverse of the correct time unit.

questions.stem: What would a catalyst do to this reaction?

choices.A: Increase k by lowering activation energy
choices.B: Increase the equilibrium constant by consuming products
choices.C: Change the reaction order to zero in all cases
choices.D: Increase activation energy and speed the reaction

explanations.A: Correct. A catalyst provides a lower-energy pathway, increasing the rate constant at a given temperature without changing the equilibrium constant.
explanations.B: Catalysts do not change K by consuming products.
explanations.C: A catalyst does not automatically make the reaction zero-order.
explanations.D: Increasing activation energy would slow, not speed, the reaction.

questions.passage: A gas-phase equilibrium is studied: A(g) + B(g) ⇌ C(g), ΔH = −40 kJ/mol. At 300 K, the equilibrium mixture contains 0.20 atm A, 0.20 atm B, and 0.80 atm C. At 400 K, the mixture contains more A and B and less C. Students interpret the data using Le Châtelier’s principle and the fact that the forward reaction is exothermic.
questions.stem: What happens to product C when temperature increases from 300 K to 400 K?

explanations.D: Correct. The figure shows PC falling from 0.80 atm at 300 K to 0.45 atm at 400 K.
explanations.A: The plotted product pressure decreases, not increases.
explanations.C: PC is lower but not zero.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Equilibrium partial pressures for A(g) + B(g) ⇌ C(g) at 300 K and 400 K.

questions.stem: Why does heating favor reactants for this equilibrium?

choices.C: The forward reaction is exothermic, so heat acts like a product
choices.B: Heating always favors fewer gas molecules

explanations.C: Correct. For an exothermic forward reaction, heat can be treated as a product. Adding heat shifts equilibrium toward reactants to consume the added heat.
explanations.A: The passage gives ΔH = −40 kJ/mol, so the forward reaction is exothermic.
explanations.B: Temperature effects depend on enthalpy, not simply gas mole count.
explanations.D: Heating changes equilibrium composition; it does not remove all gas.

questions.stem: What is Kp at 300 K using the reported pressures?

explanations.A: 4 would result from an incorrect pressure ratio.
explanations.D: 0.20 is one reactant partial pressure, not Kp.

questions.stem: Adding more A at constant temperature would initially do what to Qp?

explanations.D: Correct. Qp = PC/(PAPB). Increasing PA raises the denominator, so Qp decreases and the system shifts forward.
explanations.A: Increasing a reactant lowers Qp for this expression.
explanations.B: Qp changes when a partial pressure in the expression changes.
explanations.C: Partial pressures and Qp are not negative.

questions.stem: Why does Kp change with temperature but not with adding more A at fixed temperature?

choices.A: Kp depends only on temperature for a given reaction
choices.B: Kp depends only on initial pressure
choices.C: Kp equals Qp at all times
choices.D: Adding A changes ΔH to zero

explanations.A: Correct. For a fixed reaction, the equilibrium constant is temperature-dependent. Changing composition alters Q and causes a shift, but K remains the same unless temperature changes.
explanations.B: Initial pressure can affect Q and equilibrium composition but not K at fixed temperature.
explanations.C: Q equals K only at equilibrium, not immediately after a disturbance.
explanations.D: Adding A does not change the reaction enthalpy to zero.
